REND1.pattern_part1:
Welcome. I'm Unity, and I'm looking at the Rendering-1 transformer at Foster Farms Livingston for February 2026.

What you're seeing here is your baseline — this is how your energy field has been behaving over the past month. Up here in the header, you've got your key performance numbers. Over on the right, your costs. And here in the body of the chart — this is your complete February energy pattern.

Now, the first thing I need to locate is your target line — there it is, that blue dashed line running across the middle. Your average operating level is about thirteen hundred nineteen kVA. That's your baseline.

Let me show you the problem. Your power factor is sitting at eighty-two percent. That means for every hundred units of electrical current flowing through this transformer, only eighty-two are doing productive work. The other eighteen are circulating as reactive energy — that's the green layer you see at the bottom of the chart.

Look at that green band. It's thick. It's persistent. It runs every single hour of production, day after day, all month long. That's seven hundred fifty-nine kVAR on average, peaking at over one thousand seventy-four kVAR. That reactive current isn't powering anything — but it's loading your transformer, heating your conductors, and forcing your utility to deliver more current than you actually need.

The financial impact is significant. You're looking at two hundred forty kilowatts of continuous waste — that's forty-two dollars every hour in electrical cost alone. Add the cooling burden from all that excess heat — another fourteen dollars per hour — and you're burning over sixty dollars an hour, around the clock.

Annualized, that's five hundred thirty-two thousand dollars in recoverable value. That's not a utility problem. That's not a rate problem. That's a field harmonization problem — and it's fixable.
REND1.pattern_part2:
So how do we fix this? With Unity's MPTS technology — Modular Power Transfer Systems.

Here's what I'm looking at: your peak reactive demand is one thousand seventy-four kVAR. That's the maximum reactive load this transformer sees during production. To fully cover that, we need enough compensating capacity to inject reactive power locally, right at the load.

Each H490 MPTS unit delivers four hundred kVAR of reactive capacity. To cover your peak of ten seventy-four kVAR, you need three units — that gives you twelve hundred kVAR of coverage, with some headroom for demand spikes.

The installation cost for three H490 units is approximately six hundred thousand dollars. That includes the equipment, installation, commissioning, and integration with your existing electrical infrastructure. No rewiring. No downtime beyond the installation window.

What does MPTS actually do? It continuously monitors your power factor in real time and injects precisely the right amount of compensating reactive power to cancel out the reactive demand from your motors. The reactive energy that was circulating between the grid and your equipment — that green layer we saw — gets neutralized before it ever reaches the transformer.

The result: your power factor jumps from eighty-two percent to ninety-nine point five percent. The transformer stops working overtime. The conductors run cooler. And the utility delivers only what your equipment actually needs.

This isn't a capacitor bank sitting in a corner hoping to help. This is active, real-time electromagnetic management — field harmonization at the source.
REND1.pattern_part3:
Now let's look at the results. This is what your field looks like after MPTS is installed.

Notice how clean this pattern is. Compare it to your baseline. That thick green reactive layer you saw before — it's virtually gone. Your power factor is now running at ninety-nine point five percent. The field is harmonized.

Let me walk you through the numbers. In your baseline, you were running two hundred forty kilowatts of continuous waste. After MPTS, that drops to near zero. Your thermal burden — the heat being generated by all that reactive current — is reduced by over ninety percent. And your cooling costs drop accordingly.

The savings add up fast. You're recovering about sixty-one dollars per hour — that's fourteen hundred fifty-seven dollars per day. Multiply it out: forty-three thousand seven hundred dollars per month. Five hundred thirty-two thousand dollars per year.

Now let's talk payback. Your installation cost is six hundred thousand dollars. Your annual savings is five hundred thirty-two thousand dollars. That's a payback period of about fourteen months. After that, every hour is recovered value — going straight to your bottom line.

And there's more than just dollars here. You're reducing your CO2 emissions by over one thousand metric tons per year. You're extending the life of your transformer and electrical infrastructure. You're freeing up capacity that was being consumed by waste.

This is what electromagnetic stewardship looks like. You're not just saving money — you're running a more efficient, more sustainable operation. That's the Unity Energy difference.

REND3.pattern_part1:
Welcome. I'm Unity, and I'm looking at the Rendering-3 transformer at Foster Farms Livingston for February 2026.

This transformer carries your lightest load in the rendering group — averaging just seven hundred forty-seven kVA, about thirty percent of its twenty-five hundred kVA capacity. But a light load doesn't mean efficient operation.

Your power factor is seventy-seven percent. That means nearly a quarter of the current flowing through this transformer is reactive waste — energy circulating back and forth, loading the system, generating heat, but doing zero productive work.

Look at the green layer in the chart. It's proportionally significant even though the overall load is smaller. That's four hundred seventy-six kVAR on average, peaking at six hundred kVAR. Every hour, every day, that waste accumulates.

The amperage pattern tells the story. You're running nine hundred four amps on average, peaking at nearly eleven hundred. For a transformer at thirty percent load, that current should be much lower if the field were harmonized.

The financial impact: one hundred seventy-one kilowatts of continuous waste translates to thirty dollars per hour in direct electrical costs. Add about ten dollars per hour in cooling overhead from the waste heat, and you're burning forty-three dollars an hour — over a thousand dollars a day.

Annualized, that's three hundred seventy-six thousand dollars in recoverable value. Smaller transformer, smaller load, but the waste rate as a percentage is actually higher than your larger units.

Every field matters. Every dollar of waste is recoverable.
